notes.note: Diochares, a freedman of Caesar who served as a courier of his dispatches. The letter to him is being used here to date Caesar's expected return to Rome.
The Ludi Romani, the great annual games in honour of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, held in September. Their fixed place in the calendar makes them a convenient marker for dating Caesar's arrival.
Baebius, a neighbour of Cicero's whose affairs Atticus is to handle, paired here with the neighbour Egnatius. Otherwise little known.

notes.anchor: I see that holidays have been added to my calendar
notes.note: Feriae, official days of religious rest on which public business was suspended; Caesar's calendar reforms and decreed thanksgivings kept adding to them. Cicero wants to know how many, since they bear on when work and meetings can resume.
Lucius Manlius Torquatus, the Epicurean who speaks for that school in Cicero's De Finibus; some business of his keeps Cicero from travelling to Puteoli.

Part of an estate to be divided among heirs --- coined money counted out (nummi) together with silver plate (argentum), the standard form of Roman movable wealth. The passage concerns the settlement of an inheritance.

Gaius Vestorius, a banker and businessman of Puteoli who handled financial affairs for Cicero and Atticus on the Bay of Naples. He is the natural agent to manage the auction and inheritance business here.
